tagged_story_for_tts:
Forces shaping motion.<break time=800ms/> Energy hiding in stillness.<break time=800ms/>

The word physics comes from the Greek physis, meaning "nature"—because it studies how everything moves, sticks, falls, or glows without magic.<break time=800ms/>

A ball rolls downhill because gravity pulls it like an invisible rope tied to Earth’s center.<break time=800ms/> But why doesn’t it speed up forever? Air pushes back, a silent crowd of molecules shoving against the ball’s path.<break time=800ms/> Even "empty" space isn’t empty:<break time=200ms/> particles blink in and out, borrowing energy they return before anyone notices—like a library book taken and replaced in a fraction of a second.<break time=800ms/>

Heat is just atoms jiggling.<break time=800ms/> A hot spoon’s metal vibrates faster than a cold one;<break time=200ms/> touch it, and your slower-moving skin steals some of that motion—now your fingers jiggle more.<break time=800ms/> Light bends around stars, not because it’s tired, but because mass warps space like a bowling ball on a trampoline.<break time=800ms/>

Three related subjects are quantum weirdness, chaos in pendulums, and how magnets defy touch.<break time=800ms/>
clean_story:
Forces shaping motion. Energy hiding in stillness.

The word physics comes from the Greek physis, meaning "nature"—because it studies how everything moves, sticks, falls, or glows without magic.

A ball rolls downhill because gravity pulls it like an invisible rope tied to Earth’s center. But why doesn’t it speed up forever? Air pushes back, a silent crowd of molecules shoving against the ball’s path. Even "empty" space isn’t empty: particles blink in and out, borrowing energy they return before anyone notices—like a library book taken and replaced in a fraction of a second.

Heat is just atoms jiggling. A hot spoon’s metal vibrates faster than a cold one; touch it, and your slower-moving skin steals some of that motion—now your fingers jiggle more. Light bends around stars, not because it’s tired, but because mass warps space like a bowling ball on a trampoline.

Three related subjects are quantum weirdness, chaos in pendulums, and how magnets defy touch.
